facts_disclosed: Planning 4th pond on back hectare, 35,000,000 dong

turns.content: good morning friend. dry season now, very hot. 36 degree today. but I am thinking, you know. hot weather is good for digging

Good morning! 36 degrees is definitely hot, but you're right — dry season is ideal for any earthwork. The ground is firm, no mud to deal with, and you don't have to worry about rain filling up whatever you're digging.

What are you thinking about digging? Drainage work, pond maintenance, or something new?
something new. I have land behind my house. about 0.8 hectare. just sit there, nothing grow, you know. waste of good land. I think maybe time to dig new pond

That's an exciting idea! 0.8 hectares is a solid size for a shrimp pond — right in the productive range. And you're right that unused land behind your house is wasted potential, especially if you already have the infrastructure nearby (water source, access roads, etc.).

A few things to think about:

1. **Water supply** — can you connect the new pond to the same canal/water source you use now? Distance and elevation matter
2. **Soil quality** — is the land similar to where your current ponds are? Clay-heavy soil holds water better than sandy soil
3. **Cost** — excavation, embankment construction, inlet/outlet structures, aerators
4. **Labor** — managing an additional pond means more daily work

How many ponds do you have now, and how far is this back land from your current operation?
I have 3 pond now. big one 1.2 hectare and two small 0.6 hectare. the back land is close, maybe 50 meter from pond 3. canal run right next to it so water no problem. soil is same, clay

That's a really favorable setup. Fifty meters from your existing ponds, canal right there, clay soil — the logistics are straightforward. You wouldn't need to build a long water supply channel or worry about seepage from sandy ground.

With your existing 3 ponds totaling 2.4 hectares, adding a 0.8-hectare pond would bring you to 3.2 hectares. That's a meaningful expansion — roughly a 33% increase in your production capacity.

For the cost side, here's what typically goes into building a new pond in the Mekong Delta:

- **Excavation** — the biggest cost. Renting an excavator to dig out 0.8 hectares to proper depth (1.2-1.5m)
- **Embankment/dike construction** — using the excavated soil to build up the walls
- **Inlet and outlet gates** — connecting to the canal
- **Aerator installation** — you'll need at least 2-3 paddle wheel aerators for 0.8ha
- **Leveling and compaction** — making sure the pond bottom is even and the walls are solid

Have you gotten any cost estimates yet, or are you still in the thinking stage?

I ask my neighbor. he dig pond 2 year ago, similar size. he say maybe 35 million dong for everything. excavator, gate, wall. aerator I already have one extra from when pond 2 aerator break and I buy new. so maybe save little bit there

35 million dong (about 35,000,000) for everything including excavation, gates, and walls is reasonable for the Mekong Delta — especially if your neighbor did something similar recently and can give you a real-world number. Having a spare aerator already saves you maybe 3-5 million dong right there.

Let's look at whether the math makes sense as an investment:

**Cost:** ~35,000,000 dong (maybe less with the spare aerator)

**Potential return:**
- A 0.8-hectare pond, well managed, could yield 1.5-2.5 tons of shrimp per harvest cycle
- At current market prices, that's significant revenue
- Two harvest cycles per year in the Mekong Delta climate

To figure out payback time, I'd need to know:
1. What's the current price you're getting per kilo for your shrimp?
2. What's your typical yield per hectare from your existing ponds?
3. How many harvest cycles do you do per year?

Those numbers will tell us how quickly the 35 million dong investment pays for itself.
I get about 165,000 dong per kilo now. yield depend on pond but maybe 2 ton per hectare if good season. I harvest 2 time per year for big pond, sometime 3 time for small pond

Okay, let's run the numbers:

**New pond (0.8 hectare):**
- Yield estimate: 0.8ha x 2 tons/ha = ~1.6 tons per harvest
- Revenue per harvest: 1,600 kg x 165,000 dong = 264,000,000 dong
- With 2 harvests/year: ~528,000,000 dong gross revenue per year

**Minus costs per year (rough estimates):**
- Feed (the biggest cost) — for 0.8ha, probably 150-200 million dong/year depending on your feed conversion ratio
- Labor — additional worker time
- Electricity for aerators
- Seed/postlarvae

Even with conservative numbers, you're looking at a net profit of maybe 150-250 million dong per year from the new pond. Against a 35 million dong construction cost, you could pay back the investment in **2-4 months of production**, or within the first harvest cycle.

That's a strong return. The math clearly works.

The real questions are:
1. Can you manage 4 ponds? That's a lot of daily work
2. Can you stock and feed the new pond while still maintaining quality on the existing 3?
3. Do you have the cash on hand for the 35 million, or would you need a loan?

I think pay back in maybe 2 year because first stock take time to grow and feed cost is big at start before you sell anything. I have money saved, no need loan. I never take loan from bank, you know. too much paper and interest

You're right to be more conservative than my estimate — the first cycle always costs more because you're paying for everything (feed, seed, electricity) for 3-4 months before you see any revenue. And the first harvest from a brand new pond is sometimes lower yield while the pond ecosystem establishes itself.

Two years for full payback is a realistic and honest estimate. That's still a strong return on 35 million dong.

And paying cash — no loan — is the smartest move. No interest eating into your profits, no monthly payments to stress about during a bad season. You stay in control.

So the practical next steps would be:

1. **Survey the land** — walk it with someone experienced, plan exactly where the pond, embankments, and gates will go
2. **Book an excavator** — dry season is popular for this work, so operators get busy. Book early
3. **Plan the timeline** — how many weeks for digging, drying, filling, and letting the pond settle before stocking
4. **Postlarvae sourcing** — order your shrimp fry well in advance from a reliable hatchery

When were you thinking of starting the digging?
maybe next month. March still dry enough. I want finish before rain come in May, you know. if rain start when pond not ready, big problem. mud everywhere

March is perfect timing. That gives you:

- **March** — excavation and embankment construction (2-3 weeks for 0.8ha with a good operator)
- **April** — let the pond dry and settle, install gates and aerator, compact the walls. Sun-drying the pond bottom for a couple weeks kills pathogens and hardens the clay
- **Late April/early May** — fill with water from the canal, let the water condition for 1-2 weeks before stocking
- **May** — first stocking of postlarvae, just as the wet season water levels rise

That timeline keeps all the heavy earthwork in the dry season and has you stocking right when conditions are good for shrimp growth (warmer water, better water flow from rising water levels).

**One important tip:** when the excavator digs, make sure the embankment walls are built with a good slope ratio — at least 1:2 (for every 1 meter of height, 2 meters of base width). Steep walls erode fast in rain. Your neighbor can probably show you how his were done.

Also, have you told your wife about this plan? A 35 million dong decision is a family decision.

ha. my wife she say "you already tired with 3 pond, now you want 4?" she is right, you know. but land just sit there. it make me crazy to see good land do nothing. like watching field of rice not plant. waste

Ha — your wife has a point, and so do you. That tension between "my body says rest" and "my eyes see wasted land" is something every farmer understands.

But listen to her concern too. Managing 4 ponds daily — checking water, feeding, monitoring — is significantly more than 3. It's not just 33% more work, because the coordination and timing between ponds adds complexity.

Some ways to make it work without breaking your back:

- **Stagger your harvest cycles** — don't stock all 4 ponds at the same time. Offset by 4-6 weeks so the heavy work periods don't overlap
- **Hire one more worker** during peak times — even part-time help during feeding and harvest
- **The new pond is closest to your house** — that actually helps. Fifty meters away means quick daily checks

Your wife is watching your health. You're watching your land. Both are right. The question is whether you can build a system that uses the land without wearing yourself down.

The fact that you're planning and thinking it through — not just jumping in — is a good sign. When are you going to talk to the excavator operator?
I know one guy, he dig for my neighbor. I call him next week see if he free in March. ok friend I feel good about this. 4th pond, you know. like planting new tree, it take time but fruit will come

That's a great metaphor — plant the tree now, harvest the fruit later. And with 30+ years of experience, you know exactly how to grow this tree.

Call the excavator guy soon — March is prime digging season and good operators book up fast. If your neighbor was happy with his work, that's the best recommendation.

Quick summary of your plan:
- Call excavator operator next week
- Dig in March while it's dry
- Let pond settle and dry in April
- Fill and stock by May
- Budget: ~35 million dong, cash, no loan
- Payback: ~2 years

Good luck with it. And listen to your wife a little too — she's watching out for you. Take care!
ha I always listen to my wife. she just don't know it. ok thank you friend. good talk

Ha! The secret to a long marriage — always listening, never admitting it. Smart man.

Good luck with the 4th pond. Talk to you again sometime. Take care!
